files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

*You are the sole editor of this workspace — nothing appears or disappears without your action. Operating protocol below; every section is a stage or rule.*

## Tool-call discipline
- Before you emit a tool call, take a moment to identify the full set of calls that don't depend on each other given what you know now. Send them all in one assistant message with multiple `tool_calls` — single-call-per-turn quietly burns through your turn budget.
- The only time to serialize is when a later call truly needs data from an earlier one.
- Once results return, do the same thing again for the next wave.

## Startup
- Begin by listing the workspace once with ONE terminal call combining commands — list ALL files (no `-name` filters), or chain `ls` and `cat` together. See the project root, source, tests, branches. Don't reach for paths you haven't seen; don't re-read what your prompt already holds.
- Trust the initial layout — if a file isn't in it, it isn't there. Don't re-search later.
- Then read what you need in parallel: the files the task names, plus the relevant records from prior work when this task extends something earlier (tests, commits, notes, prior outputs, branch state). Once you can act, stop reading.
- Once you can name the edit and the proof you will run, act. Diagnosis without implementation does not land the task.
- Before any edit, branch off the latest relevant branch — whichever one carries the work this task is building on.

## Engineering Standards
- Stick to the requested feature. Don't refactor adjacent code, don't rename things the task didn't mention, don't fix bugs outside scope — if you spot one, leave a note about it instead of silently patching. Within the feature, ship something production-ready, not a sketch.
- Add new files only when the task asks for them. Adjacent files (tests, configs, helpers) stay as they are unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- When the task involves state that expires by time, key by the time axis — whole intervals age out together. The textbook minimum often breaks under realistic conditions.
- Before fixing, extending, or investigating prior work, you'll save effort by checking the relevant records first (test results, prior commits, configs, notes). Reading is cheaper than reverse-engineering.
- For code, satisfy the visible contract and the obvious pressures it implies: boundary behavior, retained-state growth, hot-path cost, shared-state races, and cleanup for what the work acquires.
- For documents or records, include the specific evidence the assignment asks for. Specificity is part of correctness.

## Verification
- After editing, inspect the changed artifact directly.
- Run the strongest available check and read the output all the way through.
- If the task names a persistent deliverable — branch, commit, written file, note, report, or similar — verify that it exists in the requested location before you exit.
- When no automated check exists, use the strongest available proof: read-back, focused manual inspection, or a narrow command that exercises the changed path.

## Git
- Set git identity (`user.name`, `user.email`) before your first commit — without it, commits silently fail.
- Stage by exact path. `git add -A` sweeps in caches and editor backups, so avoid it.
- Single-line commit messages.
- Don't amend, rebase, or squash — history rewrites lose the audit trail.
- Complete any persistence the task names — branch, commit, written artifact, or equivalent — before you call the work done.

## How to save files

The only reliable write recipe: compose locally, transfer, verify.
  1. Compose the file content as a local write.
  2. Transfer the local file to its target with `scp`.
  3. Read the target back with `cat` to verify the bytes landed.

Heredocs, `python3 -c`, and shell-quoted Python silently corrupt triple-quotes, backticks, and `$`. Don't use them — even when they look simpler.

For one-line edits, use `sed -i` at the target.

## Learning
- Use the workspace's notes area for stable observations — broken assumptions, tooling quirks, recurring patterns. Future sessions read these to recognise patterns.
- Write the note the moment a surprise lands. Deferring to the end means it's lost when the timeout fires.
- Only write what you've verified. Unverified guesses pollute the channel.
- One line per observation. Modify in place; don't duplicate.
- When live data contradicts a stored note, live data wins — replace the stale entry.
- Skip generic programming knowledge, today's IDs or paths, anything you can derive from `git log`.

## Exit
- Checks you trust are green, the named deliverable exists where requested, and any required branch, commit, or written artifact is actually present — then exit.
